title: Single-Collection Design & Multi-Value Indexes
description: The universal NoSQL principle — what is accessed together should be stored together — refined with compound indexes, multi-value indexes, and the full power of SQL analytics.
introduction:
There is a universal principle in data modeling that predates any specific database: what is accessed together should be stored together. In relational systems, normalization optimizes for storage efficiency and update consistency — and then you pay for it at read time with multi-table joins. In document systems, you have a choice. You can embed related data inside a single document for fast reads, or you can store related documents separately in the same collection and use indexes to collect them.

That choice is not a philosophy decision. It is a physics decision. The right answer depends on your storage engine, your access patterns, and the size of your documents.

Consider the tradeoffs. Embedding an advisor's entire client roster inside a single advisor document gives you one-read access to the full entity graph. But when a client's portfolio value changes — which happens constantly in financial services — you are rewriting the entire advisor document for a single field update. If that document is 2KB, the overhead is negligible. If it is 200KB, you are moving 200KB of data for a 8-byte number change. On MongoDB, WiredTiger writes 32KB leaf pages regardless of how much data changed within them. On Oracle, documents under roughly 8KB are stored inline with the row in the table's data blocks. Cross that threshold and the document migrates to LOB storage — a fundamentally different I/O path with different performance characteristics. These are the physics of your storage engine, and they determine whether embedding or separating is the right model.

Single-collection design refines the universal principle by taking these physics into account. Instead of embedding everything into one massive document, you store small, focused documents — each representing a single entity like an advisor profile, a client summary, or a holding — together in the same collection. A compound index on shared attributes groups them, so a single indexed query retrieves the entire related set in one round trip. You get the locality benefit of embedding without the write amplification penalty. And because each document is small, you stay within your storage engine's sweet spot — inline storage on Oracle, efficient page utilization on MongoDB.

[svg:m3-single-table.svg]

The collection in this module — advisory_entities — stores advisors, clients, accounts, and holdings as separate documents. Each document carries a grouping key (pk) that co-locates related entities and an entity key (sk) that identifies and orders documents within the group. A compound index on (pk, sk) turns this collection into a hierarchical structure where one query retrieves an advisor with all their clients, or all holdings for a specific account. Alternate compound indexes on (gsi1pk, gsi1sk) enable different access patterns — like finding all holdings of a given stock symbol across every account — without duplicating data or maintaining separate collections.

This is where Oracle transforms the pattern. On a pure document database, single-collection design gives you fast point reads and bounded range scans within an index prefix. That is all. Cross-group analytics require application-side aggregation. Joining entity types requires multiple queries stitched together in code. On Oracle, you keep the locality benefits but gain the full SQL engine: joins across entity types within the collection, GROUP BY and window functions for cross-group analytics, CTEs for multi-step computations, and JSON_TABLE for shredding arrays into relational rows. And you get CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX — full B-tree indexes on every element inside a JSON array, queryable individually or combined. MongoDB's parallel arrays restriction prohibits compound indexes on two array fields. Oracle has no such limitation.

By the end of this module, you will have explored the entity collection, created compound indexes for multiple access patterns, built multi-value indexes on arrays and combined them in queries that other document databases physically cannot execute, and run SQL analytics that would require a separate analytical database on any other platform. The universal principle, refined by storage engine physics, amplified by SQL convergence.
checkpoint: One collection. Four entity types. Compound index query returns an advisor with all their clients in a single round trip — plus SQL joins, aggregation, and multi-value indexes.

exercises.title: Explore the Entity Collection
exercises.description: See how multiple entity types co-exist in one collection, query by grouping key, and filter by entity key prefix.
exercises.explanation:
This is single-collection design in practice. Four entity types — advisors, clients, accounts, and holdings — live together in the advisory_entities collection. Each document carries two key fields that enable hierarchical access. The **grouping key (pk)** co-locates related entities: everything belonging to advisor 5 shares `pk = 'ADVISOR#5'`, so one indexed query retrieves the advisor profile and all associated clients in a single range scan. The **entity key (sk)** distinguishes document types within the group and provides ordering: `sk = 'PROFILE'` is the advisor document, `sk = 'CLIENT#1005'` is a specific client. A prefix filter like `LIKE 'CLIENT#%'` lets you retrieve just the client documents without touching the advisor profile or any other entity type.

This is the same principle whether you are on Oracle, MongoDB, or any document database — compound indexes on shared attributes group related documents for single-query retrieval. The **entityType** field provides an explicit type discriminator, making it easy to count, filter, and aggregate by entity type across the entire collection. On Oracle, the cost-based optimizer treats these JSON path expressions exactly like relational column predicates, choosing index scans or full scans based on data statistics.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT DISTINCT ae.data.pk.string() FROM advisory_entities ae ORDER BY 1` — see all grouping keys across the collection
- `SELECT ae.data.sk.string(), json_serialize(ae.data.data PRETTY) FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE ae.data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#3' AND ae.data.sk.string() LIKE 'CLIENT#%'` — explore a different advisor's clients
exercises.code:
-- See all entity types in one collection
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS entity_type,
       COUNT(*) AS count
FROM advisory_entities
GROUP BY data.entityType.string()
ORDER BY count DESC;

-- Query by grouping key — get an advisor and all their entities
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS type,
       data.sk.string() AS entity_key,
       json_serialize(data.data PRETTY) AS payload
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#5'
ORDER BY data.sk.string();

-- Filter by entity key prefix — get just the clients for an advisor
SELECT data.data.firstName.string() || ' ' || data.data.lastName.string() AS client_name,
       data.data.riskProfile.string() AS risk_profile,
       data.data.totalValue.number() AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#5'
  AND data.sk.string() LIKE 'CLIENT#%'
ORDER BY data.data.totalValue.number() DESC;

steps.label: Count all entity types in the collection
steps.code:
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS entity_type,
       COUNT(*) AS count
FROM advisory_entities
GROUP BY data.entityType.string()
ORDER BY count DESC;

steps.label: Query by grouping key — get an advisor and all their entities
steps.code:
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS type,
       data.sk.string() AS entity_key,
       json_serialize(data.data PRETTY) AS payload
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#5'
ORDER BY data.sk.string();

steps.label: Filter by entity key prefix — get just the clients for an advisor
steps.code:
SELECT data.data.firstName.string() || ' ' || data.data.lastName.string() AS client_name,
       data.data.riskProfile.string() AS risk_profile,
       data.data.totalValue.number() AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.pk.string() = 'ADVISOR#5'
  AND data.sk.string() LIKE 'CLIENT#%'
ORDER BY data.data.totalValue.number() DESC;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM advisory_entities WHERE json_value(data, '$.entityType') IS NOT NULL
exercises.checkpoint: You just queried a single collection containing four entity types using compound key patterns. One query, one index scan, multiple entity types — the universal principle in action.

exercises.title: Compound Indexes for Access Patterns
exercises.description: Create compound indexes on the entity collection to support primary and alternate access patterns.
exercises.explanation:
Every access pattern your application needs should resolve to an indexed lookup — a point read or a bounded range scan. The **compound index** on `(pk, sk)` supports the primary grouping pattern: retrieve all entities for an advisor, or filter within the group by entity key prefix. The second compound index on `(gsi1pk, gsi1sk)` enables an **inverted access pattern** — finding all holdings of a given stock symbol across every account in the collection. This is the same entity data, accessed through a different lens. The first index answers 'show me everything for this advisor.' The second answers 'show me every account that holds AAPL.'

Oracle's **functional indexes** on JSON path expressions are backed by real B-tree indexes and a cost-based optimizer that decides when and how to use them. The optimizer can combine multiple indexes in a single query plan using bitmap conversion, and it gathers statistics on JSON path cardinality to choose the optimal access strategy. You can create as many compound indexes as your access patterns demand — there is no arbitrary limit, no replication lag, and no storage duplication. Each index is transactionally consistent with the collection data.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT ae.data.gsi1pk.string(), COUNT(*) FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE ae.data.gsi1pk.string() IS NOT NULL GROUP BY ae.data.gsi1pk.string() ORDER BY 2 DESC` — see all alternate grouping key values and their distribution across entity types
exercises.code:
-- Primary access pattern: pk + sk (entity co-location)
CREATE INDEX idx_pk_sk ON advisory_entities (
    data.pk.string(),
    data.sk.string()
);

-- Alternate access pattern: inverted index for cross-group queries
CREATE INDEX idx_gsi1 ON advisory_entities (
    data.gsi1pk.string(),
    data.gsi1sk.string()
);

-- Use the alternate index to find all holdings for a symbol across all accounts
SELECT data.pk.string() AS account,
       data.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.gsi1pk.string() = 'SYMBOL#AAPL'
  AND data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Create compound index on pk + sk for entity co-location queries
Create alternate compound index for cross-group access patterns
Use the alternate index to find all holdings for a symbol
steps.code:
SELECT data.pk.string() AS account,
       data.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.gsi1pk.string() = 'SYMBOL#AAPL'
  AND data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_indexes WHERE index_name IN ('IDX_PK_SK', 'IDX_GSI1')
exercises.checkpoint: You just created compound indexes on JSON paths — each one enables a different access pattern against the same collection, with zero replication lag and full transactional consistency.

exercises.title: Multi-Value Indexes — Index Into Arrays
exercises.description: Create multi-value indexes on tags and sectors arrays, then query across both — something impossible on MongoDB due to the parallel arrays restriction.
exercises.explanation:
**CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX** lets Oracle build a B-tree index on every element inside a JSON array. Each array element gets its own index entry, so `JSON_EXISTS` queries on array contents resolve via fast B-tree lookup — not a full collection scan. This is critical for any domain with tags, categories, or multi-valued attributes (which is most real-world data).

Here is where document modeling tradeoffs become concrete. You could embed tags directly into the holding document (which we have done), or you could store tags as separate small documents in the collection and join them. The right answer depends on how often tags change versus how often they are queried. In this case, tags are relatively static metadata — embedding them is the right call because it avoids a join, and the multi-value index makes them individually queryable despite being embedded in an array.

The critical advantage: MongoDB prohibits compound indexes on two array fields — the **parallel arrays restriction**. Oracle has no such limitation. You can create multi-value indexes on tags AND sectors and query both in the same WHERE clause. The optimizer uses **bitmap conversion** to intersect the two indexes efficiently, meaning a query like 'find all S&P 500 holdings in the technology sector' hits two B-tree indexes and merges the results without scanning a single document that does not match.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT ae.data.data.symbol.string(), json_serialize(ae.data.data.tags) FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "growth")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'` — find growth-tagged holdings and see their full tag sets
exercises.code:
-- Create a multi-value index on the tags array
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_tags ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.data.tags.string());

-- Create a multi-value index on the sectors array
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_sectors ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.data.sectors.string());

-- Find all holdings tagged "dividend"
SELECT data.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value,
       json_serialize(data.data.tags) AS tags
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

-- Combine: find all S&P 500 holdings in the technology sector
SELECT data.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "sp500")')
  AND JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.sectors?(@ == "technology")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Create a multi-value index on the tags array
steps.code:
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_tags ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.data.tags.string());

steps.label: Create a multi-value index on the sectors array
steps.code:
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_sectors ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.data.sectors.string());

steps.label: Find all holdings tagged 'dividend'
steps.code:
SELECT data.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value,
       json_serialize(data.data.tags) AS tags
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Combine both indexes — find S&P 500 holdings in the technology sector
steps.code:
SELECT data.data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "sp500")')
  AND JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.sectors?(@ == "technology")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_indexes WHERE index_name IN ('IDX_TAGS', 'IDX_SECTORS')
exercises.checkpoint: You just indexed into JSON arrays with MULTIVALUE INDEX and queried across two array fields in one WHERE clause. On MongoDB, this compound index would fail — parallel arrays restriction. On Oracle, it's standard B-tree semantics.

exercises.title: SQL Analytics on Collection Data
exercises.description: Cross-entity aggregation, tag analytics, and sector exposure reports — queries that pure document databases cannot optimize.
exercises.explanation:
This is where the convergence advantage becomes undeniable. **JSON_TABLE** shreds JSON arrays into relational rows using a COLUMNS clause that maps JSON paths to typed SQL columns, turning nested array elements into first-class rows that participate in GROUP BY, SUM, window functions, and CTEs. The tag analytics query explodes each holding's tags array into individual rows, then groups and aggregates across the entire book of business. On a pure document database, this requires a multi-stage aggregation pipeline with no cost-based optimization — the database executes the stages in the order you wrote them, not the order that is fastest.

The sector exposure report goes further: it uses a **CTE (Common Table Expression)** with `SUM() OVER()` as a window function to calculate each sector's percentage of total portfolio value in a single pass. This is exactly why single-collection design is more powerful on Oracle — you model as documents for developer velocity and storage efficiency, then query as relational for analytical power. The optimizer figures out the fastest execution path. JSON_TABLE performs an implicit lateral join, meaning each row in the outer table is joined with the rows generated from its own JSON array.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT jt.tag, ae.data.pk.string() AS grouping_key, COUNT(*) FROM advisory_entities ae, JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY jt.tag, ae.data.pk.string() ORDER BY 1, 2` — see tag distribution per account grouping
exercises.code:
-- TAG ANALYTICS: Holdings count by tag (across entire book)
SELECT jt.tag,
       COUNT(*) AS holding_count,
       SUM(ae.data.data.marketValue.number()) AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae,
JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
GROUP BY jt.tag
ORDER BY total_value DESC;

-- SECTOR EXPOSURE REPORT: Market value by sector with percentage
WITH sector_values AS (
    SELECT jt.sector,
           SUM(ae.data.data.marketValue.number()) AS sector_value
    FROM advisory_entities ae,
    JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.sectors[*]' COLUMNS (sector VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
    WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
    GROUP BY jt.sector
)
SELECT sector,
       sector_value,
       ROUND(sector_value / SUM(sector_value) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_portfolio
FROM sector_values
ORDER BY sector_value DESC;

steps.label: Tag analytics — holdings count and total value by tag
steps.code:
SELECT jt.tag,
       COUNT(*) AS holding_count,
       SUM(ae.data.data.marketValue.number()) AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae,
JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
GROUP BY jt.tag
ORDER BY total_value DESC;

steps.label: Sector exposure report — market value by sector with portfolio percentage
steps.code:
WITH sector_values AS (
    SELECT jt.sector,
           SUM(ae.data.data.marketValue.number()) AS sector_value
    FROM advisory_entities ae,
    JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.sectors[*]' COLUMNS (sector VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
    WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
    GROUP BY jt.sector
)
SELECT sector,
       sector_value,
       ROUND(sector_value / SUM(sector_value) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_portfolio
FROM sector_values
ORDER BY sector_value DESC;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM (SELECT jt.tag FROM advisory_entities ae, JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.data.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY jt.tag)
exercises.checkpoint: You just ran SQL aggregations across entity types using JSON_TABLE and window functions — the analytical power of SQL applied directly to document collection data.

exercises.title: Multi-Value Index + Duality View — Convergence
exercises.description: Combine single-collection design with duality views — multi-value index identifies matches, SQL JOIN navigates relationships, duality view shapes the output.
exercises.explanation:
This is the convergence payoff — **three paradigms in one query**, one optimizer, one round trip. The multi-value index on the entity collection identifies holdings tagged 'dividend' via B-tree lookup, avoiding any collection scan. The SQL JOIN navigates from the collection entity through the relational accounts table to the client portfolio duality view, bridging the document and relational models within the same transaction. The duality view then projects the final output as a rich JSON document with nested accounts and holdings — the shape your API consumer expects.

On a polyglot architecture, this query would require three separate systems: a document store for the entity collection, a relational database for client and account data, and an aggregation layer to stitch them together. Three network round trips and a prayer that nothing changed between the reads. Here it is one SQL statement, and the cost-based optimizer decides whether to use nested loop joins, hash joins, or merge joins based on real-time statistics from both the collection and the relational tables.

This pattern is the architectural thesis of the entire workshop: **model the domain, project the access, let the optimizer handle the physics**.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(cp.data PRETTY) AS client_portfolio FROM advisory_entities ae JOIN accounts a ON a.account_id = TO_NUMBER(REPLACE(ae.data.pk.string(), 'ACCOUNT#', '')) JOIN client_portfolio_dv cp ON json_value(cp.data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = a.client_id WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "growth")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'` — change the tag filter to 'growth' to see different client portfolios
exercises.code:
-- Query: Find clients whose holdings include "dividend" tagged securities
-- using the multi-value index on the entity collection,
-- then join to the relational model for the full client document
SELECT json_serialize(cp.data PRETTY) AS client_portfolio
FROM advisory_entities ae
JOIN accounts a ON a.account_id = TO_NUMBER(
    REPLACE(ae.data.pk.string(), 'ACCOUNT#', '')
)
JOIN client_portfolio_dv cp ON json_value(cp.data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = a.client_id
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';
exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.data.tags?(@ == "dividend")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
exercises.checkpoint: You just combined multi-value index lookup, SQL JOIN, and duality view projection in a single query. Three paradigms. One optimizer. One round trip.
